coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots for TiO2-based photocatalysts and dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yan-Qing
Ma, De-Kun
Zhang, Yan-Ge
Chen, Wei
Huang, Shao-Ming
coredata.dc:description: N-doped nanostructured carbon materials have attracted much attention because the doping can induce new unique physical and chemical properties of carbon. In this study, we report, for the first time, that N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) can combine with rutile TiO2 and form NCQDs/TiO2 hierarchical microspheres assembled by nanorods with enhanced photocatalytic activities under visible light irradiation. As a result, the pseudo-first-order reaction constant for the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) on NCQDs/TiO2 (0.11min−1) is 215.7, 7.3 and 1.3 times much larger than that of NCQDs (0.00051min−1), TiO2 (0.015min−1), and CQDs/TiO2 (0.086min−1), respectively. The functions of NCQDs were discussed and a new synergistic photosensitized mechanism was proposed for the degradation of RhB on the as-synthesized NCQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites based on the experimental results. NCQDs were also applied as metal-free sensitizers in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) for the first time. A preliminary study shows that under one sun illumination (AM 1.5), the open circuit voltage and fill factor values reach 0.46V and 43%, respectively, achieving a power conversion efficiency of 0.13% in the proof-of-concept TiO2-based DSSCs device. The performance of NCQDs sensitized solar cells surpasses that of N-free counterpart as well. N-doping lowered the work function of CQDs, which was responsible for enhanced photocatalytic activity of NCQDs/TiO2 and better NCQDs sensitized TiO2-based solar cell performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots/TiO2
Photosensitizers
Photocatalysis
Dye-sensitized solar cell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and measurements of NCQDs and CQDs sensitized solar cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to express acknowledge for partial financial support from NSFC ( 51002107 , 21173159 , and 61106125 ), NSFC for Distinguished Young Scholars ( 51025207 ), NSFZJ ( Y4090118 ), Top Key Discipline ( 100061200119 ), ZJSTD Key Innovative Team ( 2012R10014-07 ), and ZJED Innovative Team for S. Huang.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic device structure of NCQDs-sensitized TiO2 solar cell.
XRD patterns of the products synthesized with (a) only TiCl3 and (b) TiCl3 and NCQDs.
(a) and (b) FE-SEM images of NCQDs/TiO2. (c) and (d) HRTEM images of NCQDs and NCQDs/TiO2.

(a) RhB concentration changes with irradiation time over no catalyst, NCQDs, P25, TiO2 synthesized from TiCl3, mixture of TiO2 and NCQDs, CQDs/TiO2, and NCQDs/TiO2 under visible light irradiation (λ>400nm), respectively. (b) plots of ln(C 0/C) versus t of NCQDs, TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2, respectively.
Results of photocatalytic conversion of benzyl alcohol into benzaldehyde over TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2 under visible light irradiation (λ>420nm) for 4h.

UV–visible DRS spectra (A) and plots of (αhν)2 versus hν (B) of (a) TiO2 and (b) NCQDs/TiO2. (C) Upconversion PL emission spectra of NCQDs excited under different long wavelengths ranging from 600 to 850nm. (D) PL spectra of (a) NCQDs, (b) NCQDs/TiO2, and (c) TiO2 under excitation of 420nm wavelength.
The relative energy level positions of NCQDs, CQDs, and TiO2 and proposed photocatalytic mechanism for NCQDs/TiO2 under visible light irradiation.
Current density–voltage characteristics of TiO2-based DSSCs with NCQDs and CQDs as sensitizers, respectively.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon quantum dots for TiO2-based photocatalysts and dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomaterials and Chemistry Key Laboratory, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325027, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanomaterials and Chemistry Key Laboratory, Wenzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Surface Micro and Nano Materials, Xuchang University, Xuchang, Henan 461000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Surface Micro and Nano Materials, Xuchang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped nanostructured carbon materials have attracted much attention because the doping can induce new unique physical and chemical properties of carbon. In this study, we report, for the first time, that N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) can combine with rutile TiO2 and form NCQDs/TiO2 hierarchical microspheres assembled by nanorods with enhanced photocatalytic activities under visible light irradiation. As a result, the pseudo-first-order reaction constant for the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) on NCQDs/TiO2 (0.11min−1) is 215.7, 7.3 and 1.3 times much larger than that of NCQDs (0.00051min−1), TiO2 (0.015min−1), and CQDs/TiO2 (0.086min−1), respectively. The functions of NCQDs were discussed and a new synergistic photosensitized mechanism was proposed for the degradation of RhB on the as-synthesized NCQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites based on the experimental results. NCQDs were also applied as metal-free sensitizers in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) for the first time. A preliminary study shows that under one sun illumination (AM 1.5), the open circuit voltage and fill factor values reach 0.46V and 43%, respectively, achieving a power conversion efficiency of 0.13% in the proof-of-concept TiO2-based DSSCs device. The performance of NCQDs sensitized solar cells surpasses that of N-free counterpart as well. N-doping lowered the work function of CQDs, which was responsible for enhanced photocatalytic activity of NCQDs/TiO2 and better NCQDs sensitized TiO2-based solar cell performance.
N-doped carbon quantum dots, as new sensitizers, were applied in TiO2-based visible-light-responding composite photocatalysts and dye-sensitized solar cells.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new member of carbon family, the emerging CQDs have stimulated scientists' tremendous research interest because of their unique properties including large two-photon absorption cross section [1], low cytotoxity [2], excellent biocompatibility [3], and good photoinduced electron transfer ability [4]. As a result, they have been widely applied in many fields such as bioimaging [5], sensors [6], and energy storage and conversion [4]. It is still highly desirable to improve performance and extend application range of CQDs. Recently, N-doped nanostructured carbon materials (graphene, nanotube, nanofiber, etc.) have attracted much attention because the doping can efficiently induce charge delocalization and tune the work function of carbon [7–9]. As a result, the introduction of N into CQDs, namely NCQDs, will be also expected to display enhanced performances for some given applications [10].

TiO2 has been used as a popular photocatalyst for splitting water into hydrogen fuel and environmental purification because of its low toxicity, large abundance, and good photostability [11]. However, both main polymorphs of TiO2, namely, anatase (Eg=3.2eV) and rutile (Eg=3.0eV) are only activated by ultraviolet light, which largely limit their application range. Carbon doping, combination, and hybridization have been demonstrated as efficient means to achieve visible-light-responding photocatalytic activities of TiO2 [12–14]. Especially, the use of nanostructured carbon, such as nanotubes and graphene, can further enhance photocatalytic activity of TiO2/C composite photocatalysts [15–17]. Comparatively speaking, there are only a few reports on employing N doped carbon nanostructures to fabricate N doped carbon nanostructures/semiconductors composite photocatalysts [18,19]. On the other hand, carbon nanotubes and graphene are easy to aggregate and difficult to disperse in common solvents, which will limit their wide applications [20]. Using CQDs can mitigate these problems. As a result, more recently, CQDs have been developed to fabricate CQDs/anatase composite photocatalysts [21]. However, to the best of our knowledge, NCQDs/rutile composite photocatalysts have never been reported until now.

DSSCs have attracted increasing attention as a promising alternative to silicon-based solar cells because of their lower production costs and relatively high conversion efficiency [22]. Usually, dyes for DSSCs can be roughly classified as metal complexes and metal-free organic dyes. The metal-complex dyes show relatively higher conversion efficiencies than the organic ones. However, it often contains expensive rare metals (such as Ru complexes) or need complex multistep preparation and purification process (such as metal porphyrins) [23,24]. In contrast, the organic dyes possess some superior characteristics such as lower material costs and ease of purification. However, it often suffers from severe aggregation problem, which will drastically deteriorate DSSC performance because of the quenching of a photoexcited state by the intermolecular energy and/or electron transfer [25]. Therefore it is still highly necessary to develop new-type sensitizers for DSSCs.

In this study, NCQDs/rutile TiO2 hierarchical microspheres assembled by nanorods were first synthesized through a mild hydrothermal route at 100°C, employing NCQDs and TiCl3 as reaction reagents. The as-obtained NCQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites exhibit obviously enhanced visible-light-responding photocatalytic activities than NCQDs, TiO2, and CQDs/TiO2. A new synergistic photosensitized mechanism was proposed for the as-synthesized NCQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite photocatalysts through a systematic study. Furthermore, NCQDs, for the first time, was evaluated as metal-free sensitizers for DSSCs.

NCQDs and CQDs were prepared according to our previous report [26]. TiCl3 aqueous solution (20%, dissolved in 3% of hydrochloric acid) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. The other reagents including RhB, benzyl alcohol, 4-tert-butylpyrldlne, tetrapropylammonium iodide, KI, I2 and acetonitrile were analytical grade reagents, purchased from Aladdin and used as received.

For the synthesis of NCQDs/TiO2, in a typical process, 5g of TiCl3 aqueous solution, 4g of NaCl, and 22mg of NCQDs were added into 10mL of distilled water under stirring. Then the solution was put into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a capacity of 50mL. Subsequently, the autoclave was sealed and heated at 100°C for 12h. After it was naturally cooled to room temperature, the products were washed with distilled water and absolute ethanol for several times and then dried under vacuum for further characterization. The synthetic processes of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 were the same as that of NCQDs/TiO2 except that NCQDs were not used and NCQDs were replaced with CQDs, respectively.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on a Bruker D8 Advanced X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.15418nm) at a scanning rate of 8°/min in the 2θ range of 10–70°. The measurements of C and N elements were performed on a PE 2400II CHNS/O elemental analyzer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Bruker EQUINOX 55 FT-IR spectrometer ranged from 4000 to 400cm−1. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images were taken on a Nova NanoSEM 200 scanning electron microscope. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken on a Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin high-resolution transmission electron microscope (Philips-FEI Inc.), using an accelerating voltage of 300kV. The UV–visible diffuse-reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded on a UV2501PC (Shimadzu) using BaSO4 as reference. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon Inc.) equipped with a 150W of xenon lamp as the excitation source. All of the measurements were performed at room temperature.

Photocatalytic activities of the samples were evaluated by degradation of RhB and conversion of benzyl alcohol under visible-light irradiation from a 500W Xe lamp (CHF-XM500, purchased from Beijing Lituo Science and Technology Co. Ltd.) equipped with a 400 (420) nm cutoff filter. For degradation of RhB, in every experiment, 0.1g of the photocatalysts was added to 100mL of RhB solution (1×10−5 mol/L). Before illumination, the suspensions were magnetically stirred in the dark for 8h to ensure the establishment of an adsorption–desorption equilibrium between the photocatalysts and RhB. After that, the solution was exposed to visible-light irradiation under magnetic stirring. At given time intervals, 3mL aliquots were sampled and centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst particles. Then, the filtrates were analyzed by recording variations of the absorption band maximum (553nm) in the UV–visible spectra of RhB by using a Shimadzu UV2501PC spectrophotometer. As to the conversion of benzyl alcohol, a mixture of catalyst (100mg) and benzyl alcohol (0.1mmol) was dispersed in the solvent of benzotrifluoride (1.5mL), which was saturated with pure molecular oxygen (purity >99.99%) from a gas cylinder. Then the above mixture was transferred into a 10mL of Pyrex glass bottle and stirred for 30min to make the catalyst blend evenly in the solution. After the suspensions were irradiated for 4h under magnetic stirring, the mixture was centrifuged to completely remove the catalyst particles. The remaining solution was analyzed with an Agilent gas chromatograph (GC-7820 fitted with a FFAP capillary analysis column). The conversion efficiency of benzyl alcohol to benzaldehyde was defined as follows:
conversion ( % ) = [ (
C
0
–
C
r
) /
C
0
] × 100
where C 0 is the initial concentration of benzyl alcohol and C
r is the concentration of reactant, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and measurements of NCQDs and CQDs sensitized solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: The TiO2 electrode was prepared according to previous report [27]. The as-prepared TiO2 electrode and 22mg of NCQDs (CQDs) were put into 50mL of distilled water. Then they were hydrothermally treated at 100°C for 4h. After that, the NCQDs (CQDs)-sensitized TiO2 electrodes were rinsed with absolute ethanol for several times and dried under vacuum. The liquid electrolyte contained 0.6M of tetrapropylammonium iodide, 0.1M of I2, 0.1M of KI, and 0.5M of 4-tert-butylpyrldlne in the solution of acetonitrile. The NCQDs (CQDs)-sensitized solar cells were assembled by dropping a drop of liquid electrolyte above the NCQDs (CQDs)-sensitized TiO2 porous film electrode. A platinum counter electrode was placed above it. The two electrodes were clipped together and a cyanoacrylate adhesive was used as sealant to prevent the electrolyte solution from leaking. The device structure of NCQDs-sensitized TiO2 solar cell was shown in Figure 1.

I–V measurements (Keithley 2440 source meter) were obtained by using a solar simulator (Newport) with an AM 1.5G filter under an irradiation intensity of 100mWcm−2. The light intensity was calibrated using a standard silicon photovoltaic solar cell. The active cell area was 0.15cm2.

In the current hydrothermal conditions, TiCl3 hydrolyzed and were oxidized by O2 dissolved in water [28]. As a result, rutile phase TiO2 was produced, which was confirmed by XRD pattern (JCPDS no. 21-1276) shown in Figure 2a. The addition of NCQDs into the reaction system has no obvious influence on the crystallinity and phase purity of the resultant products. As seen from Figure 2b, the products still are rutile phase TiO2. No diffraction peaks derived from NCQDs can be found, which are probably attributed to their poor crystallinity and low content. The existence of NCQDs in the products could be confirmed by elemental analysis and subsequent HRTEM observation. The elemental analysis indicates that the products contain 2.04wt% of C and 0.11wt% of N. The ratio of N to C is smaller than that of original NCQDs, suggesting that some functional groups containing N on the surfaces of NCQDs were converted into other species under hydrothermal condition. FT-IR spectrum was further used to characterize the as-synthesized NCQDs/TiO2. As shown in Figure S1a (Supporting Information), the strong and wide absorption peaks centered at 780cm−1 are due to lattice vibrations of rutile TiO2 [29], while the peaks at 1612cm−1 are associated with stretching vibrations of carboxylate group. The results show that the products consist of NCQDs and TiO2 and some functional groups containing N on the surfaces of NCQDs were converted into carboxylic functional group, which is in agreement with the results of elemental analysis. The characteristic vibration peaks of carboxyl red shifted, indicating that the surface complexation of TiO2 with the carboxylic functional group derived from the surfaces of NCQDs occurs.

The morphology and microstructure of the as-obtained NCQDs/TiO2 were studied by FE-SEM and HRTEM. As shown in FE-SEM image in Figure 3a, the products are hierarchical microspheres. From a single magnified broken microsphere (Figure 3b), it can be seen that the hierarchical microsphere is built by uniform nanorods with an average diameter of 20nm. NCQDs are difficult to discern through FE-SEM due to their very small size (Figure 3c). HRTEM was further used to reveal the elaborate structure of hierarchical NCQDs/TiO2 composites (Figure 3d). The clear lattice spacing of 0.325nm in HRTEM image is consistent with the interplanar separation of (110) plane of rutile TiO2. Some tiny particles marked with white circles correspond to NCQDs. These NCQDs were closely combined with TiO2 nanorods and formed stable NCQDs/TiO2 composites.

The photocatalytic activities of NCQDs/TiO2 composites were evaluated by the degradation of RhB dye, a common pollutant model, in water under visible light irradiation (λ>400nm). As shown in Figure 4, the photodegradation efficiency of RhB over NCQDs/TiO2 is more than 95% within 30min under visible light irradiation. However, RhB is stable and has a negligible degradation in the absence of any photocatalysts under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic activity of NCQDs/TiO2 is markedly superior to NCQDs, commercial P25, TiO2 (obtained from TiCl3 in the absence of NCQDs), and physical mixture of TiO2 and NCQDs (TiO2+NCQDs). In addition, the photocatalytic activity of NCQDs/TiO2 is better than that of N-free CQDs/TiO2 as well. As we know, most of the photocatalytic reactions follow a pseudo-first-order kinetic equation: ln(C 0/C)=kt (where C 0 and C are the equilibrium concentration of adsorption and the concentration of RhB at the irradiation time, t, respectively, and k is the apparent rate constant). Figure 4b represents the plots of ln(C 0/C) versus irradiation time of the samples. The ln(C 0/C) versus t presents a good linear relationship, which means that the photodegradation of RhB on these photocatalysts obeys the rules of a first-order reaction kinetics. The apparent reaction rate constants are 0.11, 0.00051, 0.015, and 0.086min−1 for NCQDs/TiO2, NCQDs, TiO2, and CQDs/TiO2, respectively. As a result, the photocatalytic reaction rate of NCQDs/TiO2 is 215.7, 7.3 and 1.3 times larger than that of NCQDs, TiO2, and CQDs/TiO2, respectively. The reaction rate constant of NCQDs/TiO2 far exceeds the sum of that of NCQDs and TiO2, which reflects a synergetic catalytic effect of NCQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts.

According to previous reports and the current experimental results, RhB could be self-photosensitized degradation over TiO2 [30]. In order to further understand the photocatalytic activities of NCQDs/TiO2, a colorless benzyl alcohol was selected. As shown in Figure 5, 16.8% of benzyl alcohol could be converted into benzaldehyde over NCQDs/TiO2 after 4h of visible light irradiation (λ>420nm), while the conversion efficiency was only 7.4% for TiO2 and 0 for NCQDs. It should be pointed out that TiO2 derived from TiCl3 in the absence of NCQDs has almost the same morphology (Figure S2b) and crystallinity (Figure S3a) as that of NCQDs/TiO2. Rutile TiO2 (Eg=3.0eV) can not be excited by wavelength of above 420nm. Although benzyl alcohol can be converted into benzaldehyde over TiO2 through the excitation of surface complex formed between benzyl alcohol and TiO2 [31], NCQDs/TiO2 shows obviously higher photocatalytic activity. The above results further confirm that NCQDs was responsible for enhanced visible-light-responding photocatalytic activities of TiO2.

In order to reveal the functions of NCQDs, UV–visible DRS and PL spectra of NCQDs/TiO2 were studied in detail. Figure 6A describes UV–visible DRS of TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2, respectively. TiO2 shows strong absorption in the UV region, while NCQDs/TiO2 displays obvious visible light absorption ranging from 400 to 700nm except for strong UV absorption. It was reported that Ti3+ ions can arouse optical absorption at λ>600nm [32]. However, as can be seen from Figure 6A, there is no typical absorption at λ>600nm, suggesting that no residual Ti3+ ions exist in the samples. Therefore visible light absorption of NCQDs/TiO2 was ascribed to NCQDs. Figure 6B represents a plot of (αhν)2 versus the photon energy (hν), from which the band gap energies of TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2 are estimated to be 3.05 and 3.04eV, respectively. This result shows that TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2 has almost the same band gap energy. The band gap of TiO2 was not narrowed after combination with NCQDs, suggesting that a “dyade” structure between NCQDs and TiO2 was not produced, either [33]. Previous work showed that the upconversion emission of CQDs was responsible for visible-light-responding photocatalytic activities of CQDs/anatase composite photocatalysts [21]. However, this is not the case for the present NCQDs/TiO2 photocatalysts system. As shown in Figure 6C, the upconversion emission wavelength of NCQDs is larger than 420nm under excitation of different long wavelengths. Therefore upconversion emission of NCQDs is noneffective to excite photocatalysis of TiO2. Considering all the above facts, NCQDs probably play a role of photosensitizer. In order to further support this conclusion, PL spectra of NCQDs, TiO2, and NCQDs/TiO2 were comparatively studied. As shown in Figure 6D, NCQDs display a strong and wide emission peak centered at 494nm under visible light excitation of 420nm (Figure 6D-a), while the PL emission of NCQDs/TiO2 greatly weakened under the same excitation conditions (Figure 6D-b). TiO2 itself has negligible emission. These results show that the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in excited NCQDs is inhibited by TiO2 in NCQDs/TiO2 composites. In other words, photogenerated electrons in excited NCQDs were injected into TiO2. According to previous reports, the LUMO level of CQDs is in the range of 4.2–4.4eV and electron rich N-doping could further lower the work function of carbon [8,20]. The conduction band (CB) potential of rutile is 4.45eV versus absolute vacuum scale [34]. Therefore, in theory, electron transfer from NCQDs to rutile is thermodynamically favorable. Both the results of PL spectra and theoretic analysis further confirm the role of NCQDs as photosensitizers. It should be pointed out here that the close contact between NCQDs and TiO2 is necessary to optimize their performance as photosensitizers. This point can be confirmed by the aforementioned fact that the photocatalytic activity of physical mixture of NCQDs and TiO2 is obviously inferior to that of NCQDs/TiO2 because the latter formed stable heterojunction between NCQDs and TiO2 but the former did not do.

The next question is why the photocatalytic activity of NCQDs/TiO2 is better than that of N-free CQDs/TiO2. It should be pointed out here that CQDs//TiO2 has almost the same morphology (Figure S2a) and crystallinity (Figure S3b) as that of NCQDs/TiO2. Consequently, N doping is responsible for enhanced photocatalytic activity of NCQDs/TiO2. It has been reported that N doping can lower the work function of carbon nanomaterials [35]. The relative energy level alignment of rutile TiO2, CQDs, and NCQDs were shown in Figure 7. The lower work function of NCQDs than CQDs will produce much larger barrier between NCQDs and TiO2. When excited by visible light, photogenerated electrons more efficiently transfer to CB of TiO2 and subsequently convert into other reactive oxidative species. As a result, NCQDs/TiO2 displays higher photocatalytic activity than that of N-free CQDs/TiO2. Therefore N doping lowers the work function of CQDs, which is probably main reason for enhanced photocatalytic activity of NCQDs/TiO2.

Recently, graphene quantum dots and N-doped graphene have been successfully applied as metal-free sensitizers and counter electrodes in DSSCs [36,9], respectively. However, NCQDs as photosensitizers for DSSCs have never been reported. Here we show that NCQDs are potential sensitizers and superior to CQDs for TiO2-based DSSCs for the first time. Figure 8 shows the representative current density–voltage characteristics of NCQDs and CQDs sensitized TiO2-based solar cells under simulated AM 1.5 irradiation, respectively. CQDs-sensitized TiO2-based solar cell exhibited a short circuit current (Jsc) of 0.33mAcm−2, an open circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.37V, a fill factor of 28%, and overall power conversion efficiency of 0.03%, while NCQDs sensitized the same solar cell exhibited a Jsc of 0.69mAcm−2, a Voc of 0.46V, a fill factor of 43%, and overall power conversion efficiency of 0.13%. The results show that NCQDs sensitized solar cell displays better performance than N-free counterpart. It was noted that the same TiO2 solar cell in the absence of any photosensitizers has a Jsc of 0.2mAcm−2, which is obviously lower than those of CQDs and NCQDs sensitized solar cells. The results also well supported the above conclusions that CQDs and NCQDs played a role of photosensitizers in CQDs/TiO2 and NCQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic system. Although the present efficiency of NCQDs sensitized TiO2 solar cell is lower, it is hopeful that it will be further improved through controlling the concentration of N doping into CQDs, surface chemistry of NCQDs, the amount of NCQDs, and so on. The relevant research is in progress.

We have developed a facile hydrothermal route to synthesize hierarchical NCQDs/TiO2 microspheres assembled by TiO2 nanorods and NCQDs. The as-synthesized NCQDs/TiO2 composites showed a synergetic enhanced photocatalytic activity, compared with single NCQDs and TiO2. The pseudo-first-order reaction constant for the degradation of RhB on NCQDs/TiO2 is 215.7 and 7.3 times larger than that of NCQDs and TiO2, respectively. The results of photocatalytic conversion of colorless benzyl alcohol into benzaldehyde, UV–visible DRS spectra, PL spectra, together with theoretic analysis show that NCQDs act as a role of photosensitizers in NCQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts. Compared with N-free CQDs, NCQDs showed enhanced photocatalytic activity and better DSSCs performance when they were applied in TiO2-based composite photocatalysts and DSSCs, which was attributed to their lower work function induced by N doping. The present study shows that NCQDs, as new metal-free sensitizers, may have many promising applications in the fields of solar energy conversion and utilization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to express acknowledge for partial financial support from NSFC (51002107, 21173159, and 61106125), NSFC for Distinguished Young Scholars (51025207), NSFZJ (Y4090118), Top Key Discipline (100061200119), ZJSTD Key Innovative Team (2012R10014-07), and ZJED Innovative Team for S. Huang.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.nanoen.2013.07.010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Journal of the American Chemical Society

Journal of the American Chemical Society

The Journal of Physical Chemistry C
Journal of the American Chemical Society
Journal of the American Chemical Society
The Journal of Physical Chemistry B
Journal of the American Chemical Society
Journal of the American Chemical Society

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yanqing Zhang received her Bachelor degree from Wenzhou University, China in 2010. Her current research is focused on luminescent carbon dots. Her thesis topic is “Properties adjustment and applications of carbon quantum dots”. In addition, she has published several articles on some famous international journals.

Dekun Ma received his Ph.D. from University of Science and Technology of China in 2007. He is currently an associate professor of Wenzhou University. He studied as a visiting scholar at Domen-Kubota Lab, Tokyo University, Japan during 2012.08–2013.02. He has been working in the research area of nanomaterials over 8 years. His current research interests focus on photo-functional materials including carbon quantum dots for bioimaging and dye-sensitized solar cell and Bi-based semiconductors for visible light-driven photocatalysis.

Yange Zhang received her Ph.D. in 2007 from University of Science and Technology of China. She visited the Chinese University of HongKong in 2008 as a research associate. She has been an associate professor in Key Laboratory for Micro-Nano Energy Storage and Conversion Materials of Henan Province, Institute of Surface Micro and Nano Materials, Xuchang University in 2008. Her research is focused on the chemical synthesis and applications of nanomaterials.

Wei Chen was born in 1985 in China, and studied chemistry in Nankai University. He received his Ph.D. (2010) under the supervision of Prof. Yadong Li in Tsinghua University, Beijing. And then he worked as an assistant professor in Wenzhou University. Two years later, he became an associate professor of Chemistry. His research interests are on nanostructured metals and oxides, with special emphasis on targeted synthesis and applications in catalysis.

Shaoming Huang received his Ph.D. from Nankai University. He became associate professor of Chemistry after two years postdoctoral research in Nanjing University. He worked as Royal Society KCWong Fellow in Sussex University (2006), senior research scientist in CSIRO from 1997–2002 and senior scientist in Duke University from 2002–2005. He has been Qianjiang Professor in Wenzhou University since 2005, and currently he is Dean of the Faculty of Chemistry & Material Engineering, Wenzhou University. He has been working in the research area of nano-structured carbon materials over 15 years. His current research interests focus on micro/nano structured functional materials.
